State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660           322-3901            322-6441              322-6444

September 19, 1980


Karen A. Kurta
Siple & Orrock
P.O. Box 3738
Ventura, CA 93006

Dear Ms. Kurta:

     This is in response to your letter of September 4,
1980, to Thomas Houston, Chairman of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. In that letter you ask whether your
law firm must stop representing clients in dealings with the
City of San Buenaventura because one of the partners in the
firm, Dennis Orrock, is a member of the City Council. The
City Attorney of San Buenaventura has advised Mr. Orrock
that he believes that although the Political Reform Act of
1974 requires only that Mr. Orrock disqualify himself from
participating in city decisions involving the firm's clients,
other provisions of law such as Government Code Section 1090
and the Code of Professional Ethics operate to prohibit such
representation.

     This agency interprets and enforces only the Political
Reform Act. I therefore cannot advise you concerning the
application of either Government Code Section 1090 or the
Code of Professional Ethics. It is true, however, as I told
you in our telephone conversation of August 26, 1980, that
the Political Reform Act would not restrict the law firm
from representing clients in dealings with the City. It
merely prohibits Mr. Orrock from either participating as a
councilmember or representing one of the firm's clients with
respect to a city decision which would foreseeably have a
material financial effect on the client, provided that the
client has paid or promised so much to the firm within the
previous twelve months that Mr. Orrock's pro rata share is
$250 or more. See Government Code Sections 82030, 87100 and
87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(e).

Karen Kurta
September 19, 1980
Page Two


If you have any further questions concerning the Political
Reform Act, please do not hesitate to call me again.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Sarah T. Cameron
Deputy Chief for
Conflicts of Interest
Legal Division

STC:plh

LAW OFFICES

Style & Orrock                                                                Lincoln's Inn

A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION                                          950 COUNTY SQUARE DRIVE
                                                                     P. O. BOX 3738
RANDOLPH E SIPLE                                                    VENTURA, CALIFORNIA 93006
R. DENNIS ORROCK                                                    (805) 644-1838
DANIEL A. HIGSON
KAREN A. KURTA


                                                September 4, 1980


Thomas Houston
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Dear Sir:

     This letter is to request a written opinion regarding
the propriety of continued representation of a client.
The facts are as follow:

     In August or September of 1976, Buenaventura Terminal
Corporation (hereinafter BTC) engaged Randolph D. Siple
of the law firm of Siple & Orrock, a Professional Corporation,
to represent its interests in connection with a dispute between
BTC and the County of Ventura over operation of an oil storage
facility.  The corporation does not have a local representative.

     As part of the resolution of that conflict, it was agreed
that steps would be taken to annex the facility to the
City of San Buenaventura.  Conversations with the City
Attorney revealed that the facility was within an area
of multiple City/County islands and annexation would be
in accordance with the contemplated expansion of the City
boundaries.

     In July of 1979, this firm began annexation proceedings
on behalf of BTC.  All normal steps were taken and all
normal fees paid.  Due to the nature of the facility,
much ground work had to be done, the undersigned and Mr.
Siple, on behalf of Siple & Orrock, a Professional Corporation,
were in continuing communication with the City Planning staff
and additional expenses were incurred by BTC including a fire
protection study requested by the City Fire Chief.

Thomas Houston
Fair Political Practice Commission
September 4, 1980
Page Two


R. Dennis Orrock, a stock holder in this firm, was sworn in
as a member of the City Council in January 1980.

In July of 1980, as we were attempting to resolve a conflict
over proposed City Fire Department requirements, we
received the attached letter from the City Attorney dated
July 15, 1980, containing his opinion that we were in a
conflict situation due to Mr. Orrock's position as a
Councilman.

At approximately the same time, we learned that the County
Board of Supervisors had petitioned the Local Agency Formation Commission (hereinafter LAFCO) for annexation of this
parcel as part of an island annexation pursuant to Municipal
Organization Act of 1977 (GC 35000 et seq.).

As involuntary annexation under LAFCO would not require
our active participation, we felt any conflict question
had been resolved.

However, it has recently come to our attention that the
City is actively attempting to block the annexation and
to redraw the lines of the proposed island to exclude a
portion of our client's property.

We feel our client is being singled out and treated unfairly
and will be at a decided disadvantage if not represented
during the annexation proceedings.

The City has taken the position that we must withdraw as
counsel for BTC and is presently refusing to discuss the
annexation with us. (See attached letter from City Attorney
dated August 2, 1980.)

Mr. Orrock, as a stock holder of this firm, has not directly
participated in any of the dealings with BTC and, as
Councilman, intends to abstain from any discussion or
votes on the annexation when and if it comes before the
City Council. Mr. Orrock has no financial interest in
BTC nor does any stock holder, associate, or employee of this
firm.

We feel there is no conflict of interest herein and
any appearance of impropriety can be avoided merely by
Mr. Orrock abstaining from discussion and vote.

The firm clientele includes many local businessmen who
necessarily will have periodic dealings with the City,
be it zoning, signs, licensing, operating permits, or the
like. While we naturally substituted out of all cases

Thomas Houston
Fair Political Practices Commission
September 4, 1980
Page Three


pending against the City when Mr. Orrock became a Councilman,
we do not feel that we must also cease to represent any
client who has routine dealings with the City.

As the annexation is a pending matter, we would appreciate
as prompt an opinion as possible.

Very truly yours,
SIPLE & ORROCK,
A Professional Corporation

[signature]
KAREN A. KURTA

KAK/cjd
Enclosures

CITY OF
San Buenaventura
MISSION SAN BUENAVENTURA • FOUNDED 1782

July 15, 1980

R. Dennis Orrock, Esq.
Siple and Orrock
Post Office Box 3733
Ventura, CA 93006

Re: Ted's Sizzling Steaks, National Equipment
Lease Covenant of Mutual Parking, and
Other Matters

Dear Dennis:

I have received in the mail today your letter dated
July 14, 1980, forwarding an executed original and one copy
of what is apparently a proposed form of "Mutual Covenant
Re Parking" designed to comply with condition No. 28
of Resolution No. A753, relating to planned development
permit No. PD-148. That condition requires that a "Mutual
Parking Agreement be recorded by both properties subject to
the review and approval of the City Attorney and the City
Planner."

I am taking no action on the documents that you
submitted, and I am returning them to you herewith.

I first received correspondence from you on this matter
by [illegible] dated June 12, 1980,
forwarding a proposed form of "Mutual Covenant Re Parking."
I have not taken action with respect to that matter other
than to attempt, first of all, to ascertain from the
Planning Division what this related to, since your
communication was the first specific knowledge that I had
with regard to the matter. Further, I have asked the
Planning Division to supply to me forms of reciprocal
parking agreements that we have approved in the past.
I have those materials.

I avoided taking any action with regard to your request,
because not only did I wish to find out what it was all
about, but in view of the fact that you, as you are well
aware, are a member of the City Council, I felt the fact

post office box 99 • ventura, california • 93001 [805] 648-7881

R. Dennis Orrock
July 15, 1980
Page Two

that you were corresponding on behalf of a client who
was having dealings with the City required some consideration
of Government Code Sections 1090 et seq., and of the ethics
rules relating to this kind of a matter. In addition,
I have been engaged in a number of items that are more
pressing from the standpoint of the legal priorities of
the City and was on vacation from approximately June 26
through July 7. I mention this by way of background as to
why I have not written sooner and why I have taken no action
since receiving your earlier letter of June 12, 1980.

In the meantime, I must point out that it has come
to my attention that apparently the firm of Sipple & Orrock
is representing the Buenaventura Terminal Corporation in
connection with a proposed annexation, zone change, and
conditional use permit. This came to my attention on only 8
when Bob Leiten and Debby Millais of the planning staff
wished to consult with me with regard to certain aspects
of what was pending before the Planning Commission that
evening with regard to the Buenaventura Terminal Corporation
application. I mention the Buenaventura Terminal
Corporation matter because it involves the same problem
as any other situation in which either you or a representative
of your law firm is representing a client in a matter
involving a supposedly arms length transaction with the
City.

I have not had time to fully review the conflict of
interests aspects of this matter, but in view of what is
taking place and the letter received today, I think I
should respond promptly and let you know my position.

First, I think that if you review Government Code
Sections 1090 et seq., and the cases decided under those
sections, you will see, at a minimum, that there may be a
potential conflict under those sections. In any matter
which might be construed to be contractual in nature
(and a Conditional Use Permit, for example normally has
conditions imposed which must be accepted by the
applicant and which are enforceable under the principles
of contract), there is the potential that the entire
transaction will be illegal and void if falling within
these sections. For one thing, and from the City's point
of view and as its legal advisor, that is a risk that I
certainly do not believe is worth taking. Nor do I believe
that Government Code Section 1091 (b) (5), relating to
"remote interests" and indicating that the situation of
a governmental officer being "an attorney of the contracting
party" constitutes a "remote interest," would apply to the

R. Dennis Orrock
July 15, 1980
Page Three

situation where the governmental officer, or his firm,
is actively representing the contracting party in the
negotiations or in proceedings with or before the governmental
body of which the attorney is also an officer. In other
words, while I am not aware of any case law on the subject,
I am reasonably confident that the appropriate interpretation
of that provision would be that there is a "remote interest"
if it just so happens that an attorney member of the board
represents the applicant or contracting party on unrelated
matters. But I would not expect that provision to permit
the attorney or his firm to actively represent an applicant
or contracting party before or in connection with the
transactions with the body of which the attorney is one of
the governing members.

As you are aware, Government Code sections 1090 et seq.,
except in the case of a bona fide "remote interest," do
not permit the public officer to merely abstain from
participation and voting when and if the matter comes
before the governing board, as is the case in connection
with financial conflict of interests arising under the
Political Reform Act of 1974. The law is clear that if
there is a problem under Government Code section 1090
et seq., the contracts may be illegal and void, and the
officer may even be subject to criminal penalties under
Government Code section 1097 --- whether or not there is
full disclosure.

In addition, since both you and I are attorneys, I
know I need not remind you that special rules of ethics apply
to our conduct and to the conduct of our partners and
associates. Enclosed for your ready reference is a copy
of Formal Opinion No. 1977-46 adopted by the Committee
on Professional Ethics of the State Bar of California which
we discussed approximately last November in connection
with questions that we were addressing at that time as to
whether or not your firm could continue its representation
of certain parties in litigation with the City following your
having been elected to the City Council. I believe this
opinion makes it very clear that, as a matter of the legal
ethics applicable to attorneys, it is not appropriate
either for you or for any partner or office associate of
yours to be involved in representing clients in their
dealings with the City. The obvious problem is that whether
there is in fact undue influence is irrelevant. The
potential conflict and the appearance of possible impropriety,
that persons working for the City-might slant matters or
give concessions, cannot be avoided. The ethics opinion states
the reasons and the reasoning very well and fully.
Representation either by yourself or by persons who are

R. Dennis Orrock
July 15, 1980
Page Four

partners or affiliates with your firm, of persons in dealings
or transactions with the City bear the potential of bringing
the integrity of the entire City operation into question.

I recognize that you may or may not agree with some or
a great deal of the foregoing. However, I would urge you to
review these matters carefully and to consider the appearance
of impropriety on your own part that the public will almost
certainly feel exists if you do not take prompt action to
eliminate the conflict.

Furthermore, apart from the way in which you may
construe these regulations may apply to yourself, as
an attorney and member of the Bar, I also have a responsibility,
as does each member of this office, to attempt to abide by
the rules of ethics to the fullest extent possible and to
avoid conflicts of interest. It is a totally untenable
position for me, as attorney for the City, to be placed
in the position of having to negotiate or deal at arms
length with an individual or firm represented by an
attorney who is a member of the City Council, and one of
the seven persons to whom I am responsible under the City
Charter, or with an individual or firm represented by a
person who is a partner or affiliate with a City
Councilmember's law firm. The potential for the possible
appearance of impropriety is obvious, and I have no intention
whatever of putting myself in a position where I may be
criticized for acting in connection with a situation where
such a conflict exists. Therefore, even if you disagree
with some of what I have set forth above, and I would hope
that you would not, nonetheless, from the standpoint of
my responsibilities as City Attorney, I wish to reiterate what
I thought we made reasonably clear last fall, and that is
that neither I, nor any member or affiliate of this office,
will participate in any legal activity or
matter where either you or a representative of your firm
is representing a client in dealings with the City.

If necessary, we can discuss this entire matter with
the full City Council, but this is definitely what I feel
is necessary from the standpoint of this office should
you or your firm be involved in representing persons in
dealings or transactions with the City. It would be
preferable, and I think, in fact, required, as I am certain
you can appreciate, that while you are a member of the
Council you, and partners and affiliates of your firm,
refrain from representing persons in their dealings and
transactions with or before the City. Otherwise, a myriad
of problems are created that have to be confronted.

R. Dennis Orrock
July 15, 1980
Page Five

I have been in private practice, and I recognize that
it is difficult to turn away good legal business. Certainly
when people have the purest of motives and certainly mean
no harm, it is painful to have to decline representation
of a client. Unfortunately, the prices that people pay
for holding and serving in public office are sometimes very
substantial and not fully appreciated by the public.
Unfortunately, though, that is the price that has to be
paid, and in the present situation, I believe there is a
definite conflict problem that cannot be ignored. Please also
bear in mind the provisions of Charter Section 503 relating
to conflict of interests.

If any of the foregoing constitutes bad tidings, I
apologize for having to be the person who has to bring
them to your attention, but I think that is my responsibility
and obligation. I think that the representatives of these
clients, and of any others, in connection with dealings
with the City, must and should cease completely and immediately.
Otherwise, very serious problems are presented and must
be confronted and it seems to me that that is an entanglement
and problem that it would be much better not to get into.

Sincerely,

Donald S. Greenberg
City Attorney

DSG:jr

Enclosures

Formal opinion no. 1977-46
adopted by the Committee on
Professional Ethics of the State
Bar of California.

Subject: Attorney's conflict of
interest; representation of clients
in civil or criminal actions in
which city he or she serves as
councilman has an adverse in-
terest or position.

Digest: It is improper for an
attorney who is a member of the
city council to represent a
client being prosecuted in a
criminal matter by the city.
Disclosure may not eliminate
the impropriety. An attorney
existing contracts with the
city in which the attorney
serves or in unrelated matters
retains the clients' exploration
in retaining the attorney to
influence the vote on a matter
before the city council. [—]
opinion or conclusions of

also draft for other amend-
ed changes.

Authorities: State Bar Rule §-
102(b); ABA Code: Canons 5,9

The Committee has been
asked: (1) May an attorney
ethically defend a person in a

ing the attorney makes full dis-
closure of his interest and de-
clines to vote when the matter
is presented to the city council?
"May a partner or office associ-
ate of the attorney represent
the client in either (1) or (2)?"

Based primarily on the ethi-
cal principles governing con-
flicts of interest and appear-
ance of impropriety as applied
to public officials, the Commit-
tee has concluded that the answer to each of these three
questions is—No—.

interests, except with the writ-
ten consent of all parties con-

Consent to representation of
conflicting interests is consid-

ABA Code Canon 8 provides:

"A Lawyer should assist in
improving the legal system."

ABA Code EC 8-8 provides,

"A Lawyer who is a public
officer whether full or part-
time, should not engage in ac-
tivities in which his prosecutor
or official duties to the public
assuredly may be in conflict
with his official duties."

ABA Code Canon 9 provides:

"A Lawyer should avoid
even the appearance of profes-
sional impropriety."

In People v.Ribades, 12 Cal.3d
189 (1974), the Supreme Court
held that a city attorney with a
conflict of interest in a matter
may not defend or assist in the
defense of a person being prose-
cuted for a crime in the same
county. In reaching this result,
the court concluded that al-
though no actual conflict existed, the potential conflict was
evident and precluded effective representation. The court
also based its decision on the
wholly separate basis that in-
order for the public to have absolute confidence in the integrity and impartiality of our
official proceedings, our ethical
policy requires that public offi-
cials, not only in fact rule out
discharge their responsibilities
but also avoid, to the extent
possible, even the appearance of
impropriety.

The rationale of the People v.
Ribades decision was followed in
In re [illegible] 175 Cal 1979),
where it was decided that
[illegible]a disqualified attorney
present a client charge
violation of a city ordi-

decisions were reach

a city councilman cannot ethic-

action where the city is a party
or where a decision on

lice officers or other material
witnesses controlled by the city
and subject to control or infl u-
ence by the council, may be
tempted to slant their testimony in favor of the council
member's client.

In view of such considerations, and being of the belief
that an absolutely public official
must exercise the highest degree of care in avoiding the
appearance of impropriety and
or his improperly used the
influence of his or her public
office, it is our conclusion that
representation by an attorney
of a defendant in a criminal action being prosecuted by the
city in which the attorney
would be unethical and should
be declined at all stages.

"Ethical considerations are
essentially the same in analyzing the ethical propriety of any
representation of a client in
a contract negotiation with
the city in which the attorney
serves as a council member.

The potential of conflict of
interest and appearance of
impropriety are not eliminated
by the making of a full disclosure, and a declaration-To-vote.
When the contract comes up
for the council approval, it
is apparent that in this situation also, the attorney may be
tempted to slant or otherwise
exercise of public office to gain
advantages and concessions in
contract terms for the benefit
of his or her client. Thereupon, the attorney serving the
city in a contract negotiation
who are subject to the council's
jurisdiction, may be reluctant
to oppose the attorney's position."

(11) Use its public position to
obtain, or attempt to obtain, a
special advantage in legislative
matters for himself or for a client
when it is apparent that the
he knows or it is obvious that
such action is not in the public
interest.

(2) Base a public position to
influence, or attempt to influence, a judicial act in favor of
himself.

(3) Accept anything of value
from any person when the law-
yer knows or it is obvious that
the offer is made for the purpose of
influencing his action as a public official.

Although there may be no
actual conflict of interest in the
representation of a client in
negotiating a contract with the
city, the potential of such conflict and the danger of an appearance of impropriety of
such magnitude and public
concern as to require that such
representation be declined. In
the eyes of the public, it is
highly possible that representation in such cases would be
viewed with a suspicion that
the attorney was using his or
her position and influence
with the city to secure
extracting favorable or special
concessions for his or her client.
In furtherance of their interest
as it is to be their own. Even the
appearance of such impropriety could operate to weaken
the public's confidence in the
integrity and fidelity of its
public officials. L.A. Opinion
No. 27; supra; states.

"A lawyer's duty to the pub-
lic when holding public office

is stated by brand, quoted in
Prac. Counsel-the Code of Professional, page 43:

"When a lawyer is elected to the legislative body, he owes
the public with undivided
fidelity. His obligation as a
lawyer continues. It is improper for him, as for any
other lawyer, to represent
conflicting interests."

See also, Government Code
§87100 (prohibiting public officials from using official positions to influence governmental decisions where the public agency
have financial interest).

It is also our opinion that
where the client's motivation
is to somehow influence the
city in a contract matter, it
would be inappropriate for the
attorney to accept or continue
representation of the client
even in matters wholly unrelated to city activities. Knowing that the attorney has easy
access to and the ear of other
council members, the public
may well infer that it was some
ulterior or corrupt motive rather than the attorney's legal
merit that was the reason to
retain him or her in the
first place. See Drinker, Legal
Ethics, supra, page 119; Michi.
Opinion No. 83 (1944). Another
consequence of such representation is that the attorney
would be forced to decline to
vote when the client's contract
matter comes before the council. Regardless of whether the
client's real motivation in re-

62                                                                                                    CALIFORNIA STATE BAR

CITY OF

San Buenaventura
MISSION SAN BUENAVENTURA FOUNDED 1782

September 2, 1980


Randolph E. Siple, Esq.
Siple & Orrock
P.O. Box 3738
Ventura, California 93006

Re: Buenaventura Terminal Corporation

Dear Randy:

Ed McCombs is on vacation and Dan Pincetich, Acting City
Manager, received your letter of August 29, 1980 today and
brought it to my attention.

I would request that you make reference to my letter to
Dennis of July 15, 1980. I do not intend to read past the
first paragraph of your letter, which I have looked at and
from which I discern that you are purporting to represent
Buenaventura Termination Corporation in connection with the
merits of whether or not their property ought to be annexed
to the City of Ventura. I do not wish to read the rest of
your letter or to be exposed to any arguments or discussion
by you with respect to the merits of this matter on behalf
of Buenaventura Termination Corporation for the reasons set
forth in my letter of July 15, 1980. I think that if you
persist in attempting to represent Buenaventura Terminal
Corporation in connection with this matter, you may so
contaminate the transaction, if you have not already done
so, that it will be impossible for the City of San Buenaventura
to take any action, other than completely opposing the annexa-
tion, without there being the appearance of impropriety in
connection with the transaction --- and so you may be doing
your client a substantial disservice.

I am advising Dan Pincetich not to read or further
consider the letter, and to decline to have any further
conversation with you or with any other member or repre-
sentative of your firm in connection with this matter, or
any other item of City business, in which your firm may be
representing a person or party in dealings with the City.


POST OFFICE BOX 99 • VENTURA, CALIFORNIA • 93001 [805] 648-7881

Randolph E. Siple, Esq.                          September 2, 1980
                                                           Page Two


     I appreciate that you may disagree with my point of view
in connection with this subject. I hope that you can appreciate
as well that my point of view is not personal in any way and
that regardless of your point of view, it is my responsibility,
as the attorney for the City, to give advice to the City Council
and to the City staff as I deem is appropriate --- whether you
agree or not.

     I think that you should discuss with Dennis whether or not
you would like to have pursued, in some appropriate fashion, a
resolution of the question whether there is in fact a conflict
problem in this kind of situation. Such a consideration would
have to take into account not only the rules of ethics applic-
able to attorneys, but also Government Code Sections 1090 et
seq., the Political Reform Act of 1974, the common law relating
to conflict of interests as the same may still exist and apply
to public officials, and any other appropriate legal provisions.
I understand that you communicated to Ed McCombs that you had
been advised by a representative of the State Bar of California
that no ethical problem exists. If you obtain a written opinion
along this line, I would certainly be happy to consider it, but
I think that that does not necessarily resolve all of the issues
that may be involved, because I do not expect that such an
opinion would definitively address anything other than an
interpretation of the rules of ethics applicable to attorneys ---
and even there, that opinion may or may not be definitive.

     I think that you should discuss this matter thoroughly with
Dennis, out of fairness to him as your partner, because the
appearance of impropriety that may or may be perceived by the pub'ic
(see the reasoning in Ethics Opinion No. 1977-46) from your
persisting to attempt to act in connection with this or other
matters is most likely going to reflect on Dennis as well as
on yourself.

     If it should become necessary or desirable, I would be
prepared to recommend to the City Council that we seek an
appropriate opinion from the Office of the Attorney General
and/or perhaps initiate appropriate litigation to determine
this issue if that should be necessary. At a minimum, how-
ever, until such time as I might be clearly convinced that I
am incorrect in the position set forth in my July 15, 1980
letter, and in the position that there is an untenable conflict
of interest problem in the kind of situation here involved, my
advice to the City Manager and to the City staff is and will be

Randolph E. Siple, Esq.                          September 2, 1980
                                                          Page Three


that they ought not to have any dealings whatever with you or
with any representative or associate of your firm where you
or they may be representing a particular party or person in
connection with dealings with the City of San Buenaventura.
The same does and will apply with respect to myself and any
member or affiliate of this office.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        DONALD S. GREENBERG
                                        City Attorney

DSG:vp
cc: Dan Pincetich, Acting City Manager
    Dennis Orrock, Esq.

embedding.qa_text: September 19, 1980 Karen A. Kurta Siple & Orrock P.O. Box 3738 Ventura, CA 93006 Dear Ms. Kurta: This is in response to your letter of September 4, 1980, to Thomas Houston, Chairman of the Fair Political Practices Commission. In that letter you ask whether your law firm must stop representing clients in dealings with the City of San Buenaventura because one of the partners in the firm, Dennis Orrock, is a member of the City Council. The City Attorney of San Buenaventura has advised Mr. Orrock that he believes that although the Political Reform Act of 1974 requires only that Mr. Orrock disqualify himself from participating in city decisions involving the firm's clients, other provisions of law such as Government Code Section 1090 and the Code of Professional Ethics operate to prohibit such representation. This agency interprets and enforces only the Political Reform Act. I therefore cannot advise you concerning the application of either Government Code Section 1090 or the Code of Professional Ethics. It is true, however, as I told you in our telephone conversation of August 26, 1980, that the Political Reform Act would not restrict the law firm from representing clients in dealings with the City. It merely prohibits Mr. Orrock from either participating as a councilmember or representing one of the firm's clients with respect to a city decision which would foreseeably have a material financial effect on the client, provided that the client has paid or promised so much to the firm within the previous twelve months that Mr. Orrock's pro rata share is $250 or more. See Government Code Sections 82030, 87100 and 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(e). If you have any further questions concerning the Political Reform Act, please do not hesitate to call me again. Sincerely, Sarah T. Cameron Deputy Chief for Conflicts of Interest Legal Division STC:plh September 4, 1980 Thomas Houston Fair Political Practices Commission P. O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Dear Sir: This letter is to request a written opinion regarding the propriety of continued representation of a client. The facts are as follow: In August or September of 1976, Buenaventura Terminal Corporation (hereinafter BTC) engaged Randolph E. Siple of the law firm of Siple & Orrock, a Professional Corporation, to represent its interests in connection with a dispute between BTC and the County of Ventura over operation of an oil storage facility. The corporation does not have a local representative. As part of the resolution of that conflict, it was agreed that steps would be taken to annex the facility to the City of San Buenaventura. Conversations with the City Attorney revealed that the facility was within an area of multiple City/County islands and annexation would be in accordance with the contemplated expansion of the City boundaries. In July of 1979, this firm began annexation proceedings on behalf of BTC. All normal steps were taken and all normal fees paid. Due to the nature of the facility, much ground work had to be
